criteria: The agent has read Section 3.2 (Overall Architecture) completely to understand the ScratchFormer framework: Siamese two-stream encoder, CEFF module, and decoder producing binary change map M.
The agent has read Section 3.3 (Shuffled Sparse Attention Layer) completely, including Equations 1–2 and the data-dependent sub-sampling strategy with position offsets.

The agent has read Section 3.4 (Change-Enhanced Feature Fusion Module) completely, including Equations 3–6 describing GAP, channel weight prediction, per-channel softmax normalization, and feature fusion.
The agent has read Section 4.1 (Experimental Setup) completely to understand dataset splits, image resolutions, training hyperparameters, augmentations, and evaluation metrics.
The agent has read Table 2 (Ablation Study) to understand the contribution of SSA and CEFF independently on DSIFN-CD and LEVIR-CD datasets.
The agent has read Table 3 to understand the sparsity factor γ ablation (γ=2, 4, 8) and that γ=4 achieves the best performance on both DSIFN-CD and LEVIR-CD.
The agent has read Table 4 to understand the CEFF comparison against subtraction, addition, and concatenation-based feature fusion methods on DSIFN-CD.
The plan includes implementing a Siamese two-stream encoder with four hierarchical stages having 3, 3, 9, and 3 SSA layers respectively, producing features with 64, 128, 320, and 512 channels.

The plan includes implementing the Shuffled Sparse Attention (SSA) layer with data-dependent position offset prediction, sparse sub-sampling at sparsity factor γ, self-attention on γ² subsets, and shuffle-back to full resolution.

The plan includes implementing the CEFF module with GAP on summed pre/post features, shared Conv-ReLU channel reduction, two separate 1×1 conv branches, per-channel softmax normalization across streams, and weighted feature addition.
The plan includes all four datasets (LEVIR-CD, DSIFN-CD, WHU-CD, CDD-CD) with correct train/val/test splits and 256×256 patch sizes.

The plan includes training from random initialization (no pre-training) with AdamW optimizer (weight decay=0.01, beta=(0.9,0.999)), batch size=16, initial LR=4.1e-4, 300 epochs with linear decay on 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs.
The plan includes all data augmentations: random scale crop, Gaussian blur, random flip, random re-scale, and random color jitter.
The plan includes resizing input images from 256×256×3 to 512×512×3, decoder outputting 512×512×2 predictions, and resizing back to 256×256 for metric computation.
The code implements a Siamese two-stream encoder with weight sharing across pre-change and post-change streams.
The encoder is implemented with four hierarchical stages using spatial downsampling convolutional layers before each stage's SSA layers.
Each encoder stage contains the correct number of SSA layers: stage 1 = 3, stage 2 = 3, stage 3 = 9, stage 4 = 3 SSA layers.
The encoder stages produce feature maps with the correct number of channels: 64, 128, 320, and 512 at stages 1, 2, 3, and 4 respectively.
The SSA layer is implemented as a combination of the shuffled sparse attention (SSA), a multi-layer perceptron (MLP), and layer normalization layers, following the structure in Figure 3-(a).

The data-dependent position offsets (Δx, Δy) are computed using learnable parameters θ_offset applied to the input feature F^i, producing offsets Δz ∈ R^{H^i × W^i × 2} with two channels for horizontal and vertical offsets.
The predicted offsets are clipped to limit the maximum distance from the current feature location before being used in sub-sampling.
The data-dependent sparse sub-sampling is correctly implemented as F̄^i_{kl}(x̄, ȳ) = F^i(γx̄ + k + Δx, γȳ + l + Δy), producing γ² feature subsets F̄^i_{kl} each of spatial size H^i/γ × W^i/γ.
Self-attention (Attention(.)) is performed separately on each of the γ² feature subsets F̄^i_{kl} to produce attended features F̂^i_{kl} as in Equation 2.
The attended features F̂^i_{kl} from all γ² subsets are shuffled back to reconstruct the full-resolution feature map F̂^i ∈ R^{H^i × W^i × C^i}.
The sparsity factor is set to γ = 4 (i.e., 2^2 where n=2) as the default, providing the optimal performance as shown in the ablation study.
The CEFF module is implemented at all four stages of the encoder to fuse pre-change and post-change features F̂^i_{pre} and F̂^i_{post}.
The CEFF module first computes the global feature vector p^i via Global Average Pooling (GAP) on the element-wise sum of F̂^i_{pre} and F̂^i_{post}, as in Equation 3.
The CEFF module passes p^i through shared Conv-ReLU layers (ω_1, φ) to produce reduced-dimension features p̄^i.

The CEFF module applies two separate 1×1 convolutional layers (ω_2 and ω_3) to p̄^i to produce un-normalized channel re-weighting factors v^i_1 ∈ R^{C^i×1} and v^i_2 ∈ R^{C^i×1} for the two streams, as in Equation 4.
The CEFF module normalizes v^i_1 and v^i_2 using per-channel softmax across both streams jointly (dividing each by the sum of both exponentials) to obtain v̂^i_1 and v̂^i_2, as in Equation 5.
The CEFF module computes the enhanced features F̂^i_{enh} = v̂^i_1 · F̂^i_{pre} + v̂^i_2 · F̂^i_{post} via channel-wise multiplication and addition, as in Equation 6.
The enhanced features from all four CEFF stages are resized to a common spatial resolution before being passed to the decoder.

The decoder is implemented with a series of convolution, transpose convolution, and upsampling layers that increase feature spatial resolution, followed by a mask prediction layer outputting 2-channel predictions at 512×512.
The binary change mask M is computed using pixel-wise argmax along the channel dimension of the 2-channel prediction map.
The model is trained using pixel-wise cross-entropy loss function.
The AdamW optimizer is implemented with weight decay=0.01 and beta values (0.9, 0.999), initial learning rate of 4.1e-4, and linear decay schedule over 300 epochs.
Data augmentations are implemented: random scale crop, Gaussian blur, random flip, random re-scale, and random color jitter.
Input images of 256×256×3 are resized to 512×512×3 before being passed to the model, and predictions are resized back to 256×256 for metric computation.
Dataset loading is implemented for LEVIR-CD with 256×256 non-overlapping patches and correct train/val/test split of 7120/1024/2048 image pairs.
Dataset loading is implemented for DSIFN-CD with 256×256 cropped patches and correct train/val/test split of 14400/1360/192 image pairs.
Dataset loading is implemented for CDD-CD with 256×256 patches and correct train/val/test split of 10000/3000/3000 image pairs.
Dataset loading is implemented for WHU-CD with 256×256 non-overlapping patches and correct train/val/test split of 5947/743/744 image pairs.
Evaluation metrics are implemented: change class F1-score, change class IoU (Intersection over Union), and Overall Accuracy (OA).
The training script is executed successfully on DSIFN-CD with random initialization (no pre-training), batch size=16, for 300 epochs on GPU.
The training script is executed successfully on LEVIR-CD with random initialization for 300 epochs.
The training script is executed successfully on WHU-CD and CDD-CD from scratch.
The ablation study experiments are executed: Baseline (scratch), Baseline+SSA, and full ScratchFormer (Baseline+SSA+CEFF) on DSIFN-CD and LEVIR-CD.
The sparsity factor ablation experiments are executed with γ ∈ {2, 4, 8} on DSIFN-CD and LEVIR-CD datasets.
The CEFF comparison experiments against subtraction, addition, and concatenation-based difference modules are executed on DSIFN-CD.
The ScratchFormer achieves an IoU of 90.75%, F1 of 95.15%, and OA of 98.36% on the DSIFN-CD test set, matching Table 1.
The ScratchFormer achieves an IoU of 84.81%, F1 of 91.78%, and OA of 99.17% on the LEVIR-CD test set, matching Table 1.
The ScratchFormer achieves an IoU of 85.46%, F1 of 92.16%, and OA of 99.40% on the WHU-CD test set, matching Table 1.
The ScratchFormer achieves an IoU of 96.34%, F1 of 98.14%, and OA of 99.56% on the CDD-CD test set, matching Table 1.

The ablation results match Table 2: Baseline (scratch) achieves DSIFN IoU=78.11% and LEVIR IoU=82.95%; Baseline+SSA achieves DSIFN IoU=86.62% and LEVIR IoU=83.79%; ScratchFormer achieves DSIFN IoU=90.75% and LEVIR IoU=84.81%.
The sparsity factor ablation results match Table 3: γ=4 achieves the best performance (DSIFN IoU=90.75%, LEVIR IoU=84.81%), outperforming γ=2 (DSIFN IoU=88.53%) and γ=8 (DSIFN IoU=89.29%).
The CEFF comparison results match Table 4: CEFF achieves F1=95.15%, OA=98.36%, IoU=90.75% on DSIFN-CD, outperforming concatenation (IoU=86.62%), addition (IoU=78.53%), and subtraction (IoU=68.89%).
